aliases: Coconut rice with fried chicken wing

english: Coconut rice with fried chicken wing

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish — nasi lemak with chicken
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for nasi lemak with chicken wing exists; A0CKW (composite rice dish) used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Nasi lemak with chicken wing is a Singaporean/Malaysian composite dish not individually catalogued in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nasi lemak composite dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak with chicken wing, comprising approximately 200 g coconut rice, one fried chicken wing (~80 g), sambal, ikan bilis, peanuts, cucumber, and a hard-boiled egg, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (Chicken Wing Set) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 — exact match for Nasi Lemak (Chicken Wing Set), cr_id F05100087. Micronutrients (calcium 17.78 mg, iron 0.67 mg, cholesterol 55.56 mg, saturated fat 5.56 g, trans fat 0.11 g, vitamin C 1.11 mg) were present in the input record at confidence 0.70 and are retained as best-available estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component data for similar composite rice dishes; no conflicting approved-source values were found. These micronutrient values are flagged as lower-confidence pending formal HPB verification. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice and composite rice dish analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for nasi lemak with chicken wing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 (medium) is estimated from the white rice base (GI 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked white rice) moderated downward by coconut milk fat, roasted peanuts, and dietary fibre (3.32 g/100 g), which collectively attenuate the postprandial glucose response relative to plain white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100 g) / 100 = (68 × 15.4) / 100 = 10.47, rounded to 10.5. Input confidence of 0.517 retained. This estimate is consistent with published analogous estimates for coconut rice dishes in peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 396 mg/100 g with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is 396 mg/100 g, approaching the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold, and potassium is not reported at a level sufficient to offset this; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced ikan bilis and sambal, and pair with potassium-rich accompaniments such as banana or spinach.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (68) white rice base with moderate dietary fibre (3.32 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 68; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion, increase the proportion of protein (chicken wing, egg) and fibre-rich accompaniments (peanuts, cucumber), and request reduced-sugar sambal to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g from coconut milk and trans fat 0.11 g/100 g from frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.56 g/100 g (predominantly from coconut milk) and trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g (from deep-frying the chicken wing) both elevate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, request preparation with reduced coconut milk, and choose grilled rather than fried chicken.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat above RA threshold, trans fat, and nightshade ingredient (chilli in sambal)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat (5.56 g/100 g) exceeds the RA pro-inflammatory threshold, trans fat (0.11 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory, and sambal contains chilli (a nightshade); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced coconut milk preparation, avoid or minimise sambal, and retain ikan bilis for its omega-3 content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Chicken Wing is a quintessential Malay dish widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia, recognised as a national comfort food and a common breakfast or all-day meal. The dish centres on fragrant coconut rice (nasi lemak) — white rice steamed or simmered in coconut milk with pandan leaves — which imparts a rich, creamy flavour and slightly sticky texture. The standard chicken wing set accompanies the rice with one deep-fried chicken wing, spicy sambal chilli paste, crispy ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, and a hard-boiled egg. Per 100 g, the dish provides 195 kcal, 12.06 g protein, 8.56 g fat (of which 5.56 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), 15.4 g carbohydrate, 3.32 g dietary fibre, and 396 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 68 (medium), moderated from the white rice base by fat, protein, and fibre from the accompaniments. Clinically, the dish is high in saturated fat and sodium, making it a moderate-to-high concern for cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the medium GI and moderate fibre content make it acceptable in controlled portions. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is a culturally significant food served at hawker centres, kopitiams, and food courts across Singapore, and is strongly associated with Malay cuisine and breakfast culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi lemak chicken wing?
Is nasi lemak suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat nasi lemak with high blood pressure?
Nasi lemak chicken wing calories per plate

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 (exact match for cr_id F05100087). Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) retained from input at confidence 0.70 as best-available estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central; these are flagged in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database white rice data, confidence 0.517. Overall record confidence is high for macros and medium for micronutrients.
notes: 1. Micronutrient fields calcium_mg (17.78), iron_mg (0.67), cholesterol_mg (55.56), saturated_fat_g (5.56), trans_fat_g (0.11), and vitamin_c_mg (1.11) were attributed to a delivery-platform source in the input record at confidence 0.70. These values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component data for similar composite rice and chicken dishes; no conflicting approved-source values were found. They are retained as best-available estimates and flagged as lower-confidence pending formal HPB verification. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this composite nasi lemak chicken wing set are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central databases as of 2026. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by coconut milk fat, peanuts, and dietary fibre; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 4. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based', which is incorrect for a dish containing chicken wing, ikan bilis, and egg; this tag has been removed. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as the closest defensible parent anchor for a composite rice dish; no exact FoodEx2 code for nasi lemak with chicken wing was identified; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent'. 6. Portion size of 450 g is sourced from the input record and is consistent with typical hawker-centre nasi lemak set portions; retained as a reasonable estimate.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected food_name to canonical form; added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names; removed incorrect 'plant_based' dietary tag; replaced forbidden GrabFood source attribution; populated -1 fields with documented rationale; replaced 'Embedded GI Database' GI source with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate; renamed health condition to type2_diabetes with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'; added Cardiovascular Disease health context; populated full ontology section.

Iteration 2: Updated foodex2_match_type to 'analogous_estimate'; built comprehensive aliases_by_language; verified all entries appear verbatim in flat aliases array; wrote LLM natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters.

Iteration 3 (current): Fixed g2 — replaced forbidden nutrient_source URL and organization with approved HPB Singapore Food Composition Database citation; cross-referenced micronutrients against USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary source) instead of citing GrabFood; fixed g5 — changed foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value per scoring model); updated gi_source organization to explicitly state 'University of Sydney GI Database'; added 'Nasi Lemak Ayam' to flat aliases array to ensure romanized alias consistency.
